MENTAL HEALTH, CHAIR
GOVERNMENT OVERSIGHT AND
GOVERNANCE, SHAREHOLDER RIGHTS
AND SECURITIES TRANSACTIONS, CHAIR

Senate
California Legislature
DAN McCORQUODALE
SENATOR
TWELFTH DISTRICT

April 20, 1992


Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I would like to urge your most expeditious handling of an opinion
request recently filed with you by the City Attorney of the City
of Milpitas concerning possible conflict-of-interest issues
arising from proposed general plan and zoning amendments. The
City is stymied on taking any action until the relevant questions
arising under the Political Reform Act are resolved.

I would like to emphasize that I am not advocating any
particular position as to what the opinion should specify. I am
simply urging swift action.

Cordially,

Dan McCorquodale
Senator, 12th District

DM/ps
CC: Sandra Faithfull

City of Milpitas
455 E. Calaveras Blvd.
Milpitas, California 95035

April 2, 1992

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Counsel, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Request for Advice, Government Code Section 83114(b)
Hillside General Plan Amendment and Zoning Ordinance Revisions

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am requesting formal written advice on behalf of five City of Milpitas elected
and appointed officials, pursuant to Government Code Sec. 83114(b). The
elected officials and their mailing addresses are:

Vice Mayor James Lawson
2175 Fieldcrest Drive, Milpitas, CA 95035

Councilmember Barbara Lee
1934 Shenandoah Ave., Milpitas, CA 95035

The appointed officials are the following Planning Commissioners:

Mr. Urmas Meri
1374 Columbus Circle, Milpitas, CA 95035

Ms. Annell Spencer
1062 Nicklaus Avenue, Milpitas, CA 95035

Ms. Patricia Dixon
1933 Grand Teton Drive, Milpitas, CA 95035

On March 30, 1992, Mr. Lawson and Ms. Lee asked me to solicit this advice on
their behalf. At the Planning Commission meeting of March 30, I was requested
to solicit this advice on behalf of Mr. Meri, Ms. Spencer and Ms. Dixon.

This request relates to a pending decision by the Planning Commission and
City Council on a General Plan Amendment, zone change, and zoning
ordinance revisions for the Hillside area of the City of Milpitas. I am enclosing a

An Equal Opportunity Employer

Mr. Scott Hallabrin                                                                                                              Page 2

map prepared by the City of Milpitas Planning Division which shows the current
Hillside area and the location of the officials' real property.

A potential conflict of interest arises concerning these officials' ownership of
property near the Hillside area. As is evident from the enclosed map, none of
these officials have residences in the current or proposed Hillside area.
Therefore, these residences are not subject to the decisions on the Hillside
area. Although these residences are within 2500' of the Hillside boundary, they
are unlikely to be affected by the proposed General Plan Amendment or zone
changes since these homes are in established subdivisions and have no
"unique" characteristics which would be affected by the proposed changes.

I would, therefore, appreciate your review as to the "public generally" exception
to the regulations because the effect of zoning decisions on their financial
interests will be substantially the same as the effect on a significant segment of
the public (California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Section 18703).

The City of Milpitas has a population of approximately 52,000 and is 13.6
square miles. The incorporated Hillside area, as defined by the City's current
zoning ordinance consists of four square miles, three of which are privately
owned, and one of which is owned by Santa Clara County. All of the
individuals named in this letter are in a position to influence and participate in
making decisions related to the proposed General Plan Amendment and
Hillside Ordinance revisions. The Planning Commissioners will make
recommendations to the City Council on the General Plan Amendment, zone
change and ordinance amendments. The Councilmembers will make the final
decisions relating to adoption of the General Plan Amendment, zone change,
and Hillside Ordinance.

The development of the Hillsides has been a major land use issue for the City
as evidenced by the amount of time and study of development regulations to
date. The major issues related to Hillside Development include density,
minimum lot sizes, density transfer credits, open space easements, floor area
ratio requirements, and site and architectural controls.

The City Council on June 6, 1989, appointed an ad hoc seventeen member
Hillside Review Committee whose task was to review the City's Hillside
development regulations and make recommendations for proposed changes.
After meeting over forty times during a two and one-half year period, the
majority of the Committee forwarded a report to the City Council. A minority
report was also prepared and sent to the City Council on September 3, 1991.
The City Council directed the Planning Commission to hold public hearings and
make recommendations on the Hillside Review Committee's reports. These
hearings occurred in October and November 1991. On January 21, 1992, the
City Council directed the Planning Commission to set a hearing on the

Mr. Scott Hallabrin                                                                                                                Page 3


proposed General Plan Amendment and Hillside Ordinance text amendments.
At the January 21, 1992 meeting, the City Council directed the City Attorney to
prepare an interim ordinance imposing a moratorium on the acceptance of all
applications for development in the Hillside area. Ordinance No. 38.668 was
adopted as an urgency measure on January 23, 1992.* The moratorium was
subsequently extended to January 23, 1993 by Ordinance No. 38.669 adopted
on March 3, 1992. The proposed General Plan Amendment, zone change, and
ordinance revisions were scheduled for a hearing before the Planning
Commission on March 30, 1992. At that time, the Commission decided to
request this advice and continue the matter pending your review.

The conflict of interest laws were created to prevent a public official from having
an unfair advantage or exercising undue influence by virtue of that official's
position. The regulations interpreting the conflict of interest laws take into
consideration factors such as the impact of a decision on a significant segment
of the public and allow public officials to participate in the decision making
process even when the decision could have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the officials' interest so long as that decision affects the public
generally in substantially the same manner (2 C.C.R. §18703). The "Public
Generally" exception does not just apply to the total population of a jurisdiction,
it can apply to a significant segment of the public.

The Hillside area of Milpitas is obviously very visible and of concern to all the
citizens of the City. The interest in preserving this unique, scenic asset has
been part of the tradition and history of the entire community. The problems of
traffic congestion, commerce and development are of general concern and the
Planning Commission and City Council have attempted to balance these
concerns with those of orderly and regulated hillside development. Clearly, the
affected property owners are not representative of any particular interest group.
Their commonality is based on ownership of homes within a 2500' radius of this
hillside district.

The City would like to move forward on these matters as soon as possible and
we contemplate action as soon as this advice is received. I would greatly


*The January 21, 1992 City Council meeting was adjourned to January 23,
1992 for consideration of the proposed moratorium.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin                                                                                              Page 4


appreciate an expedited response so that I can advise the officials on whose
behalf this advice is sought. If I can provide additional information which would
be of assistance, please let me know.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Sandra S. Faithfull
City Attorney


SSF:pf
cc:    Mayor and City Council (w/o enclosure)
       Planning Commission (w/o enclosure)
       City Manager (w/o enclosure)

sections.question: Whether five City of Milpitas elected and appointed officials (Vice Mayor James Lawson, Councilmember Barbara Lee, and Planning Commissioners Urmas Meri, Annell Spencer, and Patricia Dixon) have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act in participating in decisions regarding a proposed Hillside area General Plan Amendment and zoning ordinance revisions, given their ownership of residential property within 2500 feet of the Hillside boundary.
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Milpitas seeks FPPC advice on whether five officials can participate in Hillside area zoning decisions despite owning nearby residential property, arguing the decisions affect the public generally rather than the officials' financial interests uniquely.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an incoming request (from City of Milpitas) to FPPC, not an FPPC response. Includes cover letter from State Senator Dan McCorquodale requesting expedited handling. Two documents presented: Senator's letter of April 20, 1992 and City Attorney's formal advice request of April 2, 1992. No CONCLUSION section exists as this is a request, not a response.

embedding.summary: The City of Milpitas City Attorney requests formal written advice from the FPPC on potential conflict-of-interest issues for five elected and appointed officials regarding their participation in decisions on a proposed General Plan Amendment and zoning ordinance revisions for the Hillside area, invoking the 'public generally' exception under California Code of Regulations Section 18703.
